headline: Malaysian Tiger Population Falls Below 150 Individuals
context: DWNP's 2025 national tiger survey using 1,200 camera traps estimated fewer than 150 Malayan tigers remain in the wild, down from 250 in the 2018 survey.

cards.big: The Malayan tiger is Malaysia's national symbol.
cards.sub: Fewer than 150 remain. At current decline rates, wild extinction is projected within 15-20 years.

cards.big: DWNP's enforcement budget for anti-poaching is RM18M annually.
cards.sub: India spends RM320M on tiger conservation for a similarly sized habitat. Malaysia's spend is 5.6% of India's.

cards.big: Snare traps removed from tiger habitat: 4,200 in 2024.
cards.sub: For every snare found, an estimated 3-5 remain undetected. Poaching for traditional medicine drives demand.

cards.big: The Central Forest Spine plan to connect tiger habitats was approved in 2009.
cards.sub: 16 years later, only 2 of 5 critical linkages are established.

cards.big: If our national symbol goes extinct on our watch, what does the symbol mean?
Tiger conservation is not a wildlife issue. It is a test of state capacity. A country that cannot protect 150 animals in its own forests has a governance deficit, not just an ecological one.
